text: This study assesses the readiness of law enforcement officials—investigators, prosecutors and judges—to enforce the principles of Law Number 11 of 2012, Juvenile Justice System which requires the application of restorative justice and diversion in accordance with the best interests of the child and to avoid any form of stigmatization. Using a descriptive legal method that employed a combination of library research and field interviews in Sleman district, this study finds that there are institutional challenges that must be addressed. Although a number of investigators and prosecutors have attended law enforcement workshops on JJ system, the impact of the law enforcement workshops has not gone beyond the surface level. Most of the judges have not yet understood the content of the law because they are still using the drafts of the law, not the final version. In addition, law enforcement officials still lack capacity-building training and tend to apply the traditional punitive approach to criminal behavior, while the institutional culture of law enforcement agencies is passive. The enforcement of the JJ law is further complicated by socio-legal issues. Many law enforcement officials felt apprehensive of using their discretion when enforcing the law, especially through the application of diversion. They suspected that the public would always suspect them of being corrupt or colluding with the suspect if they did not impose punishment and if they decided to let the suspect go freely, as a form of diversion. For the law to be fully enforced, the law enforcement officials are recommended to receive additional education on the child protection law, specialized juvenile detention places to be built, and extensive socialization in the community to reconcile the public’s perception of criminal enforcement with the restorative goal of the law on juvenile justice.
